title: The Big Picture: EU coal sanctions
section_path: The Big Picture: EU coal sanctions
section_path_text: The Big Picture: EU coal sanctions
summary:
No more Russian coal
The European sanctions have now taken full effect on Russian coal. We look at the current state of European seaborne coal demand and what the situation may look like going forward. By Mark Nugent

Sanctions now in place
The EU announced a ban on all new purchases of Russian coal shortly after the war started, with existing contracts allowed to be fulfilled until 10 August.

children.title: No more Russian coal
children.summary: The European sanctions have now taken full effect on Russian coal. We look at the current state of European seaborne coal demand and what the situation may look like going forward.

children.title: Sanctions now in place
children.summary: The EU announced a ban on all new purchases of Russian coal shortly after the war started, with existing contracts allowed to be fulfilled until 10 August. Since March, 16.2m tonnes of Russian coal has landed in Europe on bulk carriers.

children.title: European imports declining
children.summary: Perhaps a surprise to some, European coal imports have slowed in recent months despite the bloc's energy position worsening. The bloc imported 11.3m tonnes of coal in July, increasing by 30.7% YoY.

children.title: Demand impact taking shape
children.summary: Historically, Europe has had little to no effect on the demand intensity of seaborne coal trade. Since the war started, however, we can see the positive effect European coal imports have had on bulk carrier demand.

children.title: Indonesian capacity reduced
children.summary: This week, the Indonesian Energy and Mineral resources minister announced that 71 miners in the country failed to meet their domestic market obligations (DMOs) and 48 of them are now banned from exporting coal. As of today, coal stockpiles at Indonesian utilities remain at healthy levels so for now, any total ban seems unlikely.
